4 flows
High-level design interview prep HLD
Architecture, distributed systems trade-offs, and end-to-end system walkthroughs for classic system design rounds.
Low-level design interview prep LLD
Object modeling, extensible APIs, machine-coding structure, and follow-up handling for class-design rounds.
Data structures and algorithms interview prep DSA
Curated coding-round practice across general problem sets and company-specific question banks for array, tree, graph, DP, and mock-round prep.
AI Engineer learning path AI/ML
End-to-end AI/ML engineering from foundations through LLMs, RAG, agents, MLOps, and responsible AI for building production intelligent systems.

Curriculum → High-level design interview prep → Security, multi-region, and operations → Fault tolerance and graceful degradation

Lesson 3 of 4

Fault tolerance and graceful degradation

Healthy systems keep the core user journey alive when dependencies fail by shedding optional work and falling back to simpler behavior.

25-35 min HLD flow Security, multi-region, and operations 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

This is how you turn abstract availability goals into concrete runtime behavior.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Design degradable experiences Checkout may stay available while recommendations fail closed2. Contain failures early Use short, realistic timeouts3. Test the degraded mode Inject faults in staging and selected production paths

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing This is how you turn abstract availability goals into concrete runtime behavior. 2. Design degradable experiences Checkout may stay available while recommendations fail closed3. Contain failures early Use short, realistic timeouts4. Test the degraded mode Inject faults in staging and selected production paths
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

This is how you turn abstract availability goals into concrete runtime behavior.

What to say first

  • Name the minimal user journey that must survive.
  • Shed optional work first during incidents.
  • Use timeouts and isolation to contain blast radius.
2

Study section

Design degradable experiences

Not every feature needs the same availability target. Separate must-have functionality from enrichments such as recommendations, analytics, or avatars.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Checkout may stay available while recommendations fail closed
  • Feeds can show slightly stale content instead of erroring
  • Read-only modes can be safer than risky writes during incidents
3

Study section

Contain failures early

Timeouts, bulkheads, circuit breakers, and bounded queues keep one unhealthy dependency from dragging everything else down.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Use short, realistic timeouts
  • Pool resources per dependency or tenant when necessary
  • Fail fast when dependencies are clearly unavailable
4

Study section

Test the degraded mode

Fallback logic rots if it is never exercised. Drill dependency failure and ensure the product response is understandable to users.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Inject faults in staging and selected production paths
  • Track degraded-mode activation rate
  • Write runbooks for manual feature shedding

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Name the minimal user journey that must survive.
  • Shed optional work first during incidents.
  • Use timeouts and isolation to contain blast radius.
  • Test fallbacks instead of only describing them.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Claiming 100% availability for every feature.
  • Allowing fallback logic to silently rot.
  • Letting low-priority dependencies sit on the critical path.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • What can degrade in a food-delivery app during an outage?
  • How would you design a read-only mode for an account system?
  • Why can graceful degradation be better than aggressive retries?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Idempotency, retries, and back pressure Caching, asynchrony, and resilienceObservability and operational feedback loops Caching, asynchrony, and resilience

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: Fault tolerance and graceful degradation

Model how auth, audit, failover, and cost controls affect the request path instead of treating them as afterthoughts. Apply it to fault tolerance and graceful degradation so the trade-offs feel concrete.

0/3 saved Step 1/3: Explain the concept
Ln 1, Col 1 1 lines 0 words 0.0 KB markdown

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Explain the concept

Teach "Fault tolerance and graceful degradation" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Guardrails

  • What can degrade in a food-delivery app during an outage?
  • Design degradable experiences
  • Contain failures early
  • Name the minimal user journey that must survive.

Suggested Structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
